coredata.dc:title: Facile hydrothermal synthesis of high-performance GQD-CuO microflower composite anode for lithium-ion batteries

coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Jongmin
Jo, Yongcheol
Cho, Sangeun
coredata.dc:description: Copper oxide (CuO) has attracted substantial interest as a potential anode material for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) because of its high theoretical capacity, non-toxicity, low cost, and abundance. However, the practical application of CuO as an anode is limited by challenges such as high charge-transfer resistance, low electrical conductivity, and limited cycle stability. An effective approach to addressing these issues involves incorporating carbon-based materials but this requires complex synthesis processes. In this work, we propose an efficient synthesis method for the preparation of a graphene quantum dot (GQD)-CuO microflower (MF) composite film (G-CuO MF) via a one-pot hydrothermal process. The synergy between the high specific surface area of CuO MFs and the fast transportation of Li+ ions provided by GQDs boosts Li+ ion storage. The G-CuO MF composite anode exhibits high reversible capacity and long-term cycling stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Anode material
CuO microflower
Graphene quantum dot
Cycle stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the fabrication of the G-CuO MF film. (b) The lower- and (c) higher-magnification FE-SEM images and (d) HR-TEM image of the G-CuO MF film.
Rate performances of (a) CuO MF and (b) G-CuO MF anodes. Comparison of the cycling performances of (c) CuO MF and (d) G-CuO MF anodes at 0.2 A g−1 for 100 cycles.

Long-term cycling performances of (a) CuO MF and (b) G-CuO MF anodes at 2 A g−1. EIS spectra of CuO MF and G-CuO MF anodes (c) before and (d) after 1000 cycles (equivalent circuits are shown in the insets). (e) Contribution ratios of the capacitive and diffusion-controlled processes at different scan rates.

head.ce:title: Facile hydrothermal synthesis of high-performance GQD-CuO microflower composite anode for lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Energy & Environmental Technology, DGIST, Daegu 42988, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Energy & Environmental Technology, DGIST, Daegu 42988, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Dielectric and Advanced Matter Physics, Pusan National University, Busan 46241, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Dielectric and Advanced Matter Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper oxide (CuO) has attracted substantial interest as a potential anode material for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) because of its high theoretical capacity, non-toxicity, low cost, and abundance. However, the practical application of CuO as an anode is limited by challenges such as high charge-transfer resistance, low electrical conductivity, and limited cycle stability. An effective approach to addressing these issues involves incorporating carbon-based materials but this requires complex synthesis processes.

In this work, we propose an efficient synthesis method for the preparation of a graphene quantum dot (GQD)-CuO microflower (MF) composite film (G-CuO MF) via a one-pot hydrothermal process. The synergy between the high specific surface area of CuO MFs and the fast transportation of Li+ ions provided by GQDs boosts Li+ ion storage. The G-CuO MF composite anode exhibits high reversible capacity and long-term cycling stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are among the preferred options for powering portable consumer electronics because of their high energy density, low self-discharge rate, and high working voltage [1,2]. Graphite-based carbon materials are widely used as anode materials in commercial applications because of their abundant reserves, high electrochemical reversibility, and excellent structure stability. However, graphite's theoretical capacity is limited to 372 mAh g−1. This has spurred extensive research into advanced anode materials with higher capacities to further enhance LIB performance. CuO has attracted considerable attention as a LIB anode material because of its appealing properties, including a higher theoretical capacity than graphite, natural abundance, and environmental sustainability. However, using CuO as an anode in LIBs is hindered by its high charge- transfer resistance and limited cycling stability [3].

Here, we present a simple synthesis method for fabricating a composite anode of graphene quantum dot (GQD) and CuO microflower (MF) (denoted as G-CuO MF). The proposed G-CuO MF anode offers several advantages. Firstly, the three-dimensional (3D) CuO MFs provide a large electrode/electrolyte contact area, ensuring high specific capacity with efficient ion and electron transport. Secondly, GQDs have the characteristics of excellent electrical conductivity, quantum size effect (high surface-to-volume ratio), and abundant active sites, which can improve the electrochemical performance of LIBs. Lastly, the fabricated G-CuO MF composite anode exhibits outstanding rate performance, remarkable cycling stability, low charge-transfer resistance, and enhanced charge-transfer kinetics, which is attributed to the synergistic effect between CuO MF and GQDs.

A solution of 2.5 M NaOH (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and 0.125 M (NH4)2SO4 (Daejung Chemical, Korea) was prepared by dissolving the compounds in 50 mL of deionized water by stirring for 1 h. Next, 15 mg of carboxyl-functionalized GQDs powder (XFNANO, China) was added and fully dissolved. The resulting mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, with the Cu foam submerged in the reaction solution and maintained at 180 °C for 10 h. Afterward, the sample was rinsed several times with ethanol and deionized water. Finally, the G-CuO MF film was obtained by annealing at 500 °C in an argon atmosphere for 3 h (Fig. 1 (a)).

A typical coin-cell (CR2032) was assembled in an argon-filled glove box (MO Tech, Korea). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) measurements were performed using a battery tester system (WBCS 3000, Won-A Tech, Korea), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted with a ZIVE MP1 (Won-A Tech, Korea).

Fig. S1(a), (b) shows the field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images of the Cu foam. The Cu foam was oxidized and covered with well-distributed CuO MFs. The overall chemical reaction is summarized in Eq. (1) [4]:
(1) Cu + 4NaOH + (NH4)2S2O8 → CuO + 2Na2SO4 + 2NH3 + 3H2O

The carboxyl groups of GQDs can provide an efficient affinity for metal ions, enabling the facile construction of a composite with CuO MFs through a hydrothermal process. The FE-SEM images of the G-CuO MF film revealed that the microflowers (average diameter of about 7 μm) were formed through the self-assembly of nanoflakes (Fig. 1(b), (c)). The mass loadings of the CuO MF and G-CuO MF films were 1.26 and 1.35 mg cm−2, respectively. Fig. S2(a) displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the G-CuO MF film on a Cu foam. The Bragg diffraction peaks observed at 32.5°, 38.7°, 48.7°, 61.5°, 66.2°, and 68.1° correspond to the (002), (111), (−202), (−113), (−311), and (−220) reflection planes, respectively (JCPDS, CuO: 04–0937). However, no characteristic diffraction peaks indicating the presence of GQDs were detected because of their low amount and low diffraction intensity [5].

The chemical state of the G-CuO MF film was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The resulting C 1 s spectrum can be divided into four distinct peaks corresponding to C─C, C─O, C═O, and COOH, respectively (Fig. S2(b)). This suggests the presence of GQD with oxygen-containing functional groups. In addition, the HR-TEM analysis confirmed the morphology of the G-CuO MF film (Fig. 1(d)). The GQD exhibited a diameter of approximately 5 nm. The interplanar spacings (d-spacings) were 0.26 and 0.35 nm, assigned to the (002) and (002) planes of GQD and CuO, respectively [6]. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) revealed that the GQD content in the G-CuO MF film was 6.9 wt% (Fig. S3).

Fig. S4 displays the CV curve of the G-CuO MF film at 0.1 mV s−1. Three peaks were observed during the cathodic scan, corresponding to the reduction reaction of CuO to CuⅡ 1-
x CuⅠ
x O1-
x
/2, Cu2O, and Cu. Similarly, three peaks appeared in the anodic scan, indicating the oxidation reaction of Cu, Cu2O, and CuO.

The rate and cycling performances were evaluated by GCD tests. The pure GQD anode exhibited a low specific capacity; however, it demonstrated stable recovery capability and cycling stability, which can be attributed to the Li+ ion adsorption properties of its oxygen-containing functional groups (Fig. S5). The average discharge capacities of the CuO MF anode were 1235, 972, 542, 347, and 107 mAh g−1 at current densities of 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 5.0 A g−1, respectively (Fig. 2 (a)). On the other hand, the G-CuO MF anode delivered higher average discharge capacities of 1522, 1347, 967, 639, and 215 mAh g−1, respectively (Fig. 2(b)). When the current density returned to 0.2 A g−1, the capacity retention of the G-CuO MF anode was 81.9 % (1247 mAh g−1), whereas, for the CuO MF anode, it was 74.8 % (924 mAh g−1). These results indicate that GQDs enhance the rate capability of CuO MFs by facilitating the efficient transport of Li+ ions [7].

After 100 cycles at a low current density of 0.2 A g−1, the specific charge/discharge capacities of the CuO MF and G-CuO MF anodes were 799/730 and 1069/1055 mAh g−1, corresponding to Coulombic efficiencies of 91.3 % and 98.6 %, respectively (Fig. 2(c) and (d)). Notably, the G-CuO MF anode showed a higher capacity retention (76.2 %) than the CuO MF anode (54.1 %). The long-term cycling performances of the anodes were measured at a high current density of 2 A g−1 for 1000 cycles (Fig. 3 (a) and (b)). After 1000 cycles, the CuO MF and G-CuO MF anodes showed specific charge/discharge capacities of 566/510 and 772/747 mAh g−1, respectively, with corresponding Coulombic efficiencies of 90.1 % and 96.7 %. Moreover, the capacity retention of the G-CuO MF anode was 87.6 %, significantly higher than that of the CuO MFs anode (70.7 %). These results can be attributed to the presence of GQDs on the surface of CuO MFs; GQDs facilitate Li+ ion hopping through adjacent carboxyl groups and create short diffusion pathways at the electrode/electrolyte interface [8,9]. Fig. 3(c), (d) shows the EIS spectra of the CuO MF and G-CuO MF anodes before cycling and after 1000 cycles at 0.2 A g−1. The charge-transfer resistance (R ct) values of the CuO MF and G-CuO MF anodes before cycling were 62.3 and 28.1 Ω, respectively. After 1000 cycles, the CuO MF and G-CuO MF anodes exhibit a merged semicircle, which incorporates the solid electrolyte interphase resistance (R sei) and R ct, thus representing the overall interfacial resistance (R int). The R int (R sei + R ct) value of the G-CuO MF anode was calculated to be 101.5 Ω, which was lower than the value for the CuO MF anode (131.1 Ω). This indicates that introducing GQDs onto the surface of CuO MFs enhances the transport of electrons and ions due to the improved electrical conductivity [10]. The proportions of capacitive and diffusion-controlled contributions were calculated at different scan rates (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. 3(e), the G-CuO MF anode exhibited a higher capacitive contribution compared to the CuO MF anode. This can be attributed to the GQDs, which provide shortened pathways for Li+ ion transport [11].

We fabricated a G-CuO MF composite anode using a facile hydrothermal process. The G-CuO MF anode demonstrated a superior specific capacity, excellent long-term stability, and a lower R ct value compared with the CuO MF anode. The outstanding electrochemical performance of the G-CuO MF anode can be attributed to the synergistic effects between the GQDs and CuO MFs, which provide a high specific surface area, shortened pathways for Li+ ion transport, and enhanced electrical conductivity.

Jongmin Kim: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yongcheol Jo: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Sangeun Cho: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Jongmin Kim reports financial support was provided by National Research Foundation of Korea. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (NRF-2023R1A2C2005823).
